article_title: Falcon Heavy Returns After 560 Days to Close Out the Troubled ViaSat-3 Constellation

content_preview.title: Falcon Heavy Returns After 560 Days to Close Out the Troubled ViaSat-3 Constellation
content_preview.summary: SpaceX targets April 27 to launch ViaSat-3 F3 to the Asia-Pacific, ending Falcon Heavy's longest gap and capping a constellation that lost 90% of its first satellite to an antenna failure.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

SpaceX is targeting Monday, April 27, 2026 for the next flight of its Falcon Heavy rocket, carrying the ViaSat-3 F3 satellite to a geostationary transfer orbit from Launch Complex 39A at NASA's Kennedy Space Center, [Viasat confirmed](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/20/3277083/0/en/Viasat-Confirms-ViaSat-3-F3-Satellite-to-Launch-April-27-2026.html) in an April 20 press release. The 85-minute launch window opens at 10:21 a.m. EDT.

The mission ends an extended paus...

editor_notes.content_quality: Round-2 rewrite addresses all three round-1 findings, plus the optional info-level fixes. Word count tightened to 760 (in News range), structure intact, tone unchanged.
editor_notes.source_verification: Round-1 verification covered all 5 snapshots (Viasat press release, Spaceflight Now schedule, Spaceflight Now Atlas 5, SpaceNews ViaSat-3 F1, SpaceNews Europa Clipper). All facts in the rewrite that were not flagged in round 1 still verify. The two removed claims (April 28 backup window, B1098 center-core serial) were not in any snapshot; the bot correctly chose to drop them rather than cite outlets that aren't allowlisted (NextSpaceflight, Friends of NASA, X). The LZ-40 wording is now factually correct — see disagreement note below.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All findings resolved cleanly. The bot's disagreement on LZ-40 was factually correct and I am withdrawing my round-1 finding (see round_1_disagreements below).
editor_notes.originality: Same as round 1 — new topic, no overlap.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Round-2 rewrite is clean. The bot also improved the article on three info-level fronts (fixed-services wording, removed unsourced 6-tonne mass estimate, removed unsourced flight count claim). Reviewer's round-1 LZ-40 finding was factually wrong and the bot correctly disputed it with primary-source evidence, then improved clarity by adding 'the new Landing Zone 40, the recovery pad SpaceX co-located with launch complex SLC-40' to the sentence. Clean APPROVE — ready to merge.
round_1_findings_resolved: April 28 backup window — removed (no allowlisted source confirms it).
B1098 center-core serial — dropped from text (no allowlisted source confirms it).
Bonus: 'fixed-broadband' tightened to 'fixed services' to match Viasat press release.
Bonus: removed unsourced 'Falcon Heavy flight count rising to 12' claim.

round_1_disagreements.finding: Round-1 reviewer flagged 'Landing Zone 40' as a Spaceflight Now typo and asked for correction to 'LZ-1 and LZ-2'.
round_1_disagreements.bot_response: Bot disagreed and explained that LZ-40 is a real, operational SpaceX landing zone at SLC-40 (debuted Feb 13 2026, Crew-12), that LZ-1 was retired Aug 2025 (Crew-11 final landing), and that the original LZ-2 at LC-13 was decommissioned after NROL-77 on Dec 9 2025 when SpaceX's lease ended Dec 31 2025.
round_1_disagreements.reviewer_verification: Independently verified via Spaceflight Now and NASASpaceFlight reporting: LZ-1 final landing was Crew-11 (Aug 2025); LZ-2 (LC-13) final landing was NROL-77 (Dec 9 2025); LZ-40 first landing was Crew-12 booster B1101 (Feb 13 2026). The bot was correct. Round-1 finding is withdrawn.
round_1_disagreements.outcome: Bot's reformulation accepted: 'Landing Zone 2 and the new Landing Zone 40, the recovery pad SpaceX co-located with launch complex SLC-40' is more informative than the original wording and factually accurate.
